title: 5 Beloved Blockbusters That Almost NEVER Happened — Thank Goodness Someone Fought For Them
dek: From leaked footage that forced a studio's hand to a director firing his own leading man mid-shoot, these movies were one bad meeting away from disappearing forever.

items.heading: Back to the Future Was Rejected Over 40 Times — Then Almost Fell Apart AGAIN Mid-Shoot
items.text: Before it became a classic, Robert Zemeckis and Bob Gale's time-travel script was turned down by nearly every studio in town for not being raunchy enough. Once Universal finally greenlit it, the production hit a second crisis: original star Eric Stoltz's brooding performance wasn't working, forcing Zemeckis to fire him five weeks into filming and scramble to bring in Michael J. Fox.

images.caption: Michael J. Fox, hired to replace Eric Stoltz mid-shoot

items.heading: Die Hard Was Contractually Offered to Frank Sinatra First — At Age 70
items.text: Because Die Hard was based on a novel that was a sequel to the book behind Sinatra's 1968 film The Detective, 20th Century Fox was legally obligated to offer him the role of John McClane first. He passed, and the part was then shopped to a lineup of A-listers — including Schwarzenegger, Stallone, and Eastwood — before it finally landed on TV star Bruce Willis, a risky $5 million bet that made him an action legend.

images.caption: Bruce Willis, the TV star turned unlikely action hero

items.heading: Miramax Nearly Forced Peter Jackson to Kill a Hobbit — Then Lost Lord of the Rings Entirely
items.text: Disney-owned Miramax originally financed Jackson's Tolkien adaptation as a single two-hour film and reportedly pressured him to kill off a Hobbit character just to raise the stakes. When Jackson refused to compromise his three-film vision, Miramax let him shop the project elsewhere, and New Line Cinema's Bob Shaye famously greenlit all three movies to be filmed back-to-back.

images.caption: Director Peter Jackson, who refused to gut his Tolkien vision
The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring (2001)

items.heading: World War Z Was Falling Apart So Badly, Brad Pitt Had to Call In Reinforcements
items.text: Reports described the zombie epic's shoot as a disaster: budget overruns, a Hungarian SWAT raid over illegally imported weapons, and a first cut with an ending so broken that Paramount executives said the film simply didn't work. Pitt personally recruited Damon Lindelof to rewrite the third act, leading to seven weeks of costly reshoots that ultimately saved the $200 million production.

images.caption: Brad Pitt, who fought to fix the film's broken ending

items.heading: Deadpool Only Exists Because Someone Leaked Test Footage Fox Never Wanted Released
items.text: After years of Fox refusing to greenlight a Deadpool movie, test footage shot by director Tim Miller and starring Ryan Reynolds mysteriously leaked online in 2014, sparking a fan frenzy. Reynolds later confessed he was behind the leak himself, and as he put it, "the internet forced the studio to say, 'We're gonna make this movie,' and 24 hours later, that movie had a green light."

images.caption: Ryan Reynolds, who confessed to leaking his own test footage
